items.question: Who uses the naive Bayes conditional independence assumption?
items.answer: ML engineers building text classifiers, spam filters, medical triage baselines, and anyone who needs fast supervised classification with many features but limited data to estimate full joint distributions P(x|y).

items.question: What is the conditional independence assumption in naive Bayes?
items.answer: Given class y, features factorize: P(x|y) = ∏ᵢ P(xᵢ|y). Each feature contributes a separate class-conditional term; the joint is approximated by their product.

items.question: When is the independence assumption most justified?
items.answer: When features are weakly correlated after conditioning on y, when data is too sparse for joint estimation, or when you need a fast baseline—common in bag-of-words text and high-dimensional binary sensor data.

items.question: Where does the assumption appear in the classification formula?
items.answer: Inside the likelihood P(x|y) in Bayes' theorem: P(y|x) ∝ P(y) ∏ᵢ P(xᵢ|y). It replaces the intractable joint with a product used in argmax over classes.

items.question: Why multiply per-feature probabilities instead of modeling the joint?
items.answer: Joint P(x|y) requires estimating combinations of feature values—exponential parameters in d. Products of P(xᵢ|y) need only O(d) parameters per class, making training feasible on large feature spaces.

items.question: How do you classify a new instance under naive Bayes?
items.answer: Compute log P(y) + Σᵢ log P(xᵢ|y) for each class y (with smoothing for zeros), then predict argmax. Equivalent to argmax P(y) ∏ᵢ P(xᵢ|y) but numerically stable.

items.question: What happens when two features are strongly correlated given y but treated as independent?
items.answer: Cause: the product P(x₁|y)P(x₂|y) misrepresents the true joint P(x₁,x₂|y). Effect: P(x|y) is biased—often overconfident when redundant features repeat the same evidence—leading to miscalibrated P(y|x) even if argmax labels remain reasonable.

items.question: What happens when a feature value never appeared with class y in training (zero count)?
items.answer: Cause: unsmoothed P(xᵢ|y)=0 for that value. Effect: the entire product P(x|y) becomes zero, so class y can never win argmax for any instance containing that feature—regardless of other evidence.

items.question: What if features were truly independent given y—would naive Bayes be exact?
items.answer: Yes. If P(x|y) genuinely equals ∏ P(xᵢ|y), naive Bayes specifies the correct generative model and Bayes-optimal classification (with correct P(y) and factors). Calibration depends on correct estimation, not model bias.

items.question: What if you drop the prior P(y) and only multiply likelihood factors?
items.answer: You estimate argmax P(x|y) instead of argmax P(y|x)—equivalent to assuming uniform class priors. When P(spam)=0.01 vs P(ham)=0.99, ignoring the prior systematically favors minority classes with higher per-feature likelihood products.

items.question: How does naive Bayes compare to logistic regression for text classification?
items.answer: Naive Bayes is generative: models P(x|y) via ∏ P(xᵢ|y) and applies Bayes. Logistic regression is discriminative: models P(y|x) directly without the independence assumption. NB trains faster on sparse text and needs less data; logistic regression often wins when features interact and data is abundant.

items.question: Joint P(x|y) vs product ∏ P(xᵢ|y)—when is each preferred?
items.answer: Joint modeling is preferred with few features and ample data to capture correlations. The product form is preferred with many features or sparse data where joint estimation is impossible—accepting bias for scalability and speed.
